card.intuition: Pooling reduces spatial dimensions by aggregating a local patch into a single value. Max pooling takes the maximum (preserves dominant features, translation invariant). Average pooling takes the mean (smooth representation). Global average pooling (GAP) collapses each feature map to a scalar — common before the final FC layer.
card.analogy: Summarising a page by highlighting paragraphs: max pooling picks the most striking sentence in each paragraph (key feature), average pooling takes the gist of each paragraph. Both make the document shorter while preserving what matters.
when_to_use: After conv layers to reduce spatial size and computation
Max pooling: when presence of a feature matters more than exact location
Global average pooling: in modern architectures (ResNet) as a parameter-free alternative to flattening
gotchas: Pooling discards spatial precision — use stride-2 convolutions instead when you need learnable downsampling
Max pooling gradients pass only through the winning neuron (max backward)
Overlap between pooling windows (stride < pool_size) provides more spatial coverage

exercise.prompt: Implement 2D max pooling and average pooling.
hints: Average pooling: divide the sum of all patch values by the number of elements (pool_size²).
exercise.test_code:
X = [[1,3,2,4],[5,6,7,8],[3,2,1,0],[9,8,7,6]]

# Max pooling 2x2, stride 2
m = pool2d(X, pool_size=2, stride=2, mode='max')
assert m == [[6,8],[9,7]], f'Max pool wrong: {m}'

# Avg pooling 2x2, stride 2
a = pool2d(X, pool_size=2, stride=2, mode='avg')
assert a[0][0] == (1+3+5+6)/4 == 3.75
assert a[0][1] == (2+4+7+8)/4 == 5.25

# stride=1
m2 = pool2d(X, pool_size=2, stride=1, mode='max')
assert len(m2) == 3 and len(m2[0]) == 3
print('PASS')
